jsonLdWeb.name: Wind Chill Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate wind chill temperature and frostbite risk using the National Weather Service formula.

acceptedAnswer.text: Wind chill describes how cold it feels on exposed skin when wind is factored in. Moving air removes heat from your body faster than still air. A temperature of 20°F with 20 mph wind feels like 4°F.
The NWS formula is: WC = 35.74 + 0.6215T - 35.75V^0.16 + 0.4275TV^0.16, where T is temperature in °F and V is wind speed in mph. This formula is valid for temps at or below 50°F and wind above 3 mph.

mainEntity.name: At what wind chill is it dangerous?

acceptedAnswer.text: Below -20°F wind chill, frostbite can occur in 30 minutes. Below -45°F, frostbite can occur in 10 minutes. Below -60°F, frostbite can happen in 5 minutes or less. Always cover exposed skin.

mainEntity.name: Does wind chill affect pipes freezing?

acceptedAnswer.text: No, wind chill does not lower the actual temperature of objects below the ambient air temperature. Pipes freeze based on actual air temperature, not wind chill. However, wind can speed up heat loss from poorly insulated pipes.

mainEntity.name: What is the lowest wind chill ever recorded?

acceptedAnswer.text: Wind chills below -100°F have been calculated. Mount Washington, NH recorded a wind chill of -103°F in 2004. Antarctica has seen even more extreme values with temperatures of -80°F and high winds.

The current NWS wind chill formula does not account for humidity. However, moisture on skin can increase heat loss. The formula was updated in 2001 to use a model based on heat loss from exposed facial skin.

howItWorks:
How Wind Chill Is Calculated

This calculator uses the official National Weather Service wind chill formula, updated in 2001.

WC = 35.74 + 0.6215T - 35.75V0.16 + 0.4275TV0.16
  • T = Air temperature in °F
  • V = Wind speed in mph
  • Valid for temperatures ≤ 50°F and wind speeds ≥ 3 mph
  • Based on heat loss from exposed facial skin in a walking scenario
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is wind chill?

Wind chill is the perceived decrease in air temperature felt by the body due to the flow of air. Wind strips heat from exposed skin faster than calm air, making it feel colder than the actual temperature.

How is wind chill calculated?

The NWS formula: WC = 35.74 + 0.6215T - 35.75V^0.16 + 0.4275TV^0.16, where T is °F and V is mph. Valid for temps at or below 50°F and wind speeds above 3 mph.

At what wind chill is it dangerous?

Frostbite risk increases dramatically below -20°F wind chill (30 min), becomes serious below -45°F (10 min), and is immediate below -60°F (5 min or less). Always cover all exposed skin in extreme cold.

Does wind chill affect pipes freezing?

No. Wind chill only affects how cold it feels to living tissue. Objects cannot cool below the actual air temperature regardless of wind. However, wind does accelerate heat transfer from warm pipes.

What is the lowest wind chill ever recorded?

Mount Washington, NH recorded -103°F wind chill in January 2004. Antarctic research stations have recorded even more extreme effective values due to extremely low temperatures combined with high winds.

Does humidity affect wind chill?

The NWS wind chill formula does not include humidity as a variable. It is based purely on temperature and wind speed, modeled on heat loss from exposed facial skin. Wet skin loses heat faster, but this is not captured in the index.
